section.title: The Burnt Offering
section.sectionRationale: Leviticus opens the first great collection of sacrificial law, the instructions for the burnt offering, which the LORD speaks to Moses from the newly erected Tent of Meeting at the end of Exodus. This is the offering wholly given to God, and it sets the pattern followed by the offerings that come after it.
section.thesis: The burnt offering teaches Israel that approach to a holy God requires an unblemished substitute, the laying on of hands, the shedding of blood, and a total surrender of the whole animal to God, whether the worshiper brings a costly bull or the humble bird of the poor.
section.frameNote: Three movements, one for each kind of offering: the burnt offering from the herd, from the flock, and from the birds, each ending in the same phrase, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

units.label: The offering from the herd
units.note: an unblemished male, hands laid on its head

verses.text: Then the LORD called to Moses and spoke to him from the Tent of Meeting, saying,
“Speak to the Israelites and tell them: When any of you brings an {{korban|offering}} to the LORD, you may bring as your offering an animal from the herd or the flock.
If one's offering is a {{olah|burnt offering}} from the herd, he is to present an unblemished male. He must bring it to the entrance to the Tent of Meeting for its acceptance before the LORD.
He is to lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering, so it can be accepted on his behalf to {{kipper|make atonement}} for him.
And he shall slaughter the young bull before the LORD, and Aaron's sons the priests are to present the blood and sprinkle it on all sides of the altar at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
Next, he is to skin the burnt offering and cut it into pieces.
The sons of Aaron the priest shall put a fire on the altar and arrange wood on the fire.
Then Aaron's sons the priests are to arrange the pieces, including the head and the fat, atop the burning wood on the altar.
The entrails and legs must be washed with water, and the priest shall burn all of it on the altar as a burnt offering, an offering made by fire, a {{nichoach|pleasing aroma}} to the LORD.

units.label: The offering from the flock
units.note: sheep or goats, the same pattern

verses.text: If, however, one's offering is a burnt offering from the flock, from the sheep or goats, he is to present an unblemished male.
He shall slaughter it on the north side of the altar before the LORD, and Aaron's sons the priests are to sprinkle its blood against the altar on all sides.
He is to cut the animal into pieces, and the priest shall arrange them, including the head and fat, atop the burning wood that is on the altar.
The entrails and legs must be washed with water, and the priest shall bring all of it and burn it on the altar; it is a burnt offering, an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
If, instead, one's offering to the LORD is a burnt offering of birds, he is to present a turtledove or a young pigeon.
Then the priest shall bring it to the altar, twist off its head, and burn it on the altar; its blood should be drained out on the side of the altar.
And he is to remove the crop with its contents and throw it to the east side of the altar, in the place for ashes.

He shall tear it open by its wings, without dividing the bird completely. And the priest is to burn it on the altar atop the burning wood. It is a burnt offering, an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

korban.gloss: An offering, from a root meaning to draw near. The word itself teaches that sacrifice exists to bring the worshiper near to God, not merely to dispose of an animal. Every offering in Leviticus is a qorban, a means of approach to the LORD.

olah.gloss: The burnt offering, from a root meaning to go up. The whole animal, except the hide, ascends to God in smoke, so nothing is retained for the worshiper. It pictures total, unreserved consecration offered to the LORD.

kipper.gloss: To atone, to cover or make amends. The laying on of hands identifies the worshiper with the animal, so that its death is accepted in his place, covering the offense that separated him from God.

nichoach.gloss: A pleasing or soothing aroma. The phrase describes God's acceptance of the offering, not that He needs food, but that the sacrifice, rightly offered, is received with favor rather than turned away in judgment.

basic.context: Leviticus opens with God speaking to Moses from the newly built Tent of Meeting, right where Exodus left off. The first instructions concern the burnt offering, the sacrifice that is burned up entirely as a gift to God. Whether an Israelite brought a costly bull or, if poor, only a bird, the pattern was the same: an unblemished animal, hands laid on its head, blood shed, and the whole thing given over to God.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes the actual sacrificial system God gave Israel under the old covenant; it does not prescribe animal sacrifice for believers today. Its abiding truth is that sinful people cannot approach a holy God empty handed, and that atonement requires a substitute who dies in the worshiper's place.
basic.originalLanguages: The word for offering, qorban, comes from a root meaning to draw near, showing that sacrifice exists to bring people to God. The olah, burnt offering, goes up entirely in smoke, holding nothing back. And kaphar, to atone, describes the laying on of hands that transfers the worshiper's standing onto the animal that dies for him.
basic.typology: The unblemished animal, wholly consumed, foreshadows Christ, who offered Himself without spot to God and held nothing of Himself back. The provision of a lesser offering for the poor, turtledoves or pigeons, foreshadows a gospel that excludes no one for lack of means; the same atonement is available to rich and poor alike.
basic.christ: Hebrews calls Christ's sacrifice better than the blood of bulls and goats, since His offering actually takes away sin rather than merely covering it until the next year. Where the burnt offering had to be repeated, Jesus offered Himself once for all, and gave Himself wholly, holding nothing back, exactly as the burnt offering pictured.
basic.god: God is holy, so approach to Him is never casual; a substitute must die. Yet He is also gracious, providing a way in even for the poor, since the required offering scales down to a pair of birds for anyone who cannot afford a bull or lamb. His holiness never becomes an excuse to exclude the poor from His presence.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 10:1-10 says these sacrifices could never take away sins, and that Christ came to do God's will once for all. Romans 12:1 calls believers to offer their bodies as a living sacrifice, echoing the total surrender of the burnt offering. John 1:29 calls Jesus the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.
basic.oneStory: The burnt offering teaches, at the very start of Leviticus, that a holy God can only be approached through a substitute who dies in the worshiper's place and is wholly given over to Him. That pattern of atoning substitution and total consecration reaches its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the once for all sacrifice who opens the way to God for everyone, however little they bring.

context.literary: Leviticus opens exactly where Exodus ends, with the LORD speaking from the Tent of Meeting that has just been completed and filled with His glory. The burnt offering is given first among the five major sacrifices, likely because it was the most common and most fundamental act of worship at the altar.
context.historical: Israel had just left Egypt, a land whose worship looked nothing like this: no substitutionary blood, no laying on of hands, no single God who could be approached in only one prescribed way. These instructions set Israel apart as a people who come to God only through atoning sacrifice.
context.canonical: This is the first of the five great offerings that structure the opening chapters of Leviticus, burnt, grain, peace, sin, and guilt. Each shows a different facet of what it costs, and what it means, to approach a holy God, and each is picked up and fulfilled in the New Testament's picture of Christ's sacrifice.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To instruct Israel in the exact procedure for the burnt offering, so that the whole animal, brought without blemish, is consecrated entirely to God, teaching both atonement through substitution and complete surrender to the LORD.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter prescribes an actual ritual for old covenant Israel; it does not prescribe animal sacrifice for the church today. What abides is the truth beneath the ritual: sin requires atonement by a substitute, and worship of a holy God calls for total, not partial, consecration.

debates.question: Why does the burnt offering allow birds as well as animals from the herd and flock?

views.label: A gracious provision for the poor
views.case: Verses 14 to 17 show God scaling the cost of the offering down to what even the poorest Israelite could afford, so that the wealthy and the poor alike could bring an acceptable sacrifice.

views.label: A picture of equal access before God
views.case: Some see the sliding scale as teaching that standing before God was never a matter of wealth; the humblest worshiper's bird was as fully accepted as the richest worshiper's bull.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that God's holiness never becomes a barrier that only the wealthy can cross; the way of atonement was made accessible to everyone.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key word is qorban, offering, which comes from a root meaning to draw near, revealing that sacrifice is fundamentally about approach to God rather than mere ritual. The olah, burnt offering, ascends wholly in smoke, and kaphar, atonement, describes the hand laid on the animal's head, transferring the worshiper's guilt onto the substitute that dies.

typology.type: The unblemished animal offered wholly to God
typology.fulfillment: Christ, without sin, who offered Himself entirely and once for all, Hebrews 9:14, 10:10.

typology.type: The laying on of hands, identifying worshiper with sacrifice
typology.fulfillment: The believer's union with Christ, whose death is credited to those who trust in Him, 2 Corinthians 5:21.

typology.type: The sliding scale of provision, herd, flock, or birds
typology.fulfillment: The gospel offered freely to rich and poor alike, with no one excluded for lack of means, 2 Corinthians 8:9.

study.christ: Hebrews 10:1-10 contrasts these repeated sacrifices, which could never take away sin, with Christ, who came to do God's will and offered His body once for all. The burnt offering's total consumption pictures the completeness of Christ's self giving; nothing of Himself was withheld from the Father, and nothing further needs to be added to His finished work.
study.god: God's holiness requires that no one comes before Him without atonement, yet His graciousness extends that atonement to whoever brings even the smallest offering, a pair of birds. He is not a distant deity indifferent to human need, but One who prescribes, in careful detail, exactly how sinners may safely approach Him.

crossReferences.note: These sacrifices could never take away sins; Christ came to do God's will, offering His body once for all.
Believers are called to present their bodies as a living sacrifice, echoing the burnt offering's total surrender.
John the Baptist calls Jesus the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.

study.oneStory: At the opening of Leviticus, the burnt offering teaches Israel that approach to a holy God requires an unblemished substitute wholly given over in worship. That pattern of atoning substitution and total consecration finds its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, who offered Himself once for all so that everyone, whatever they bring, may draw near to God through Him.

quiz.q: What must the worshiper do before the burnt offering is slaughtered?

options.text: Lay his hand on the head of the offering, so it may be accepted to make atonement for him.
options.note: Verse 4 describes this act of identification between worshiper and sacrifice.

options.text: Recite a written prayer of confession.
options.note: No prayer is prescribed here; the text specifies the laying on of hands.

options.text: Wash himself in the courtyard first.
options.note: Washing belongs to the priestly preparation of the offering, not the worshiper's own act before slaughter.

options.text: Nothing; the priest handles the entire process alone.
options.note: The worshiper himself lays his hand on the animal and, in most cases, slaughters it.

quiz.q: What provision does God make for someone too poor to afford an animal from the herd or flock?

options.text: He may bring a turtledove or a young pigeon instead.
options.note: Verses 14 to 17 provide this offering of birds for the burnt offering.

options.text: He is exempt from bringing any offering.
options.note: The law does not exempt the poor; it scales the offering down to what they can afford.

options.text: He must borrow money to purchase a lamb.
options.note: The text provides an alternative offering rather than requiring debt.

options.text: No provision is made for the poor.
options.note: Verses 14 to 17 explicitly provide birds as an accessible offering.

quiz.q: What happens to the burnt offering on the altar?

options.text: The whole animal, except the hide, is burned completely as an offering made by fire.
options.note: This total consumption is what distinguishes the burnt offering, olah, from other sacrifices.

options.text: Only the fat portions are burned; the rest is eaten by the priests.
options.note: That pattern describes offerings like the peace offering, not the burnt offering, which is wholly consumed.

options.text: The animal is set free after the ritual.
options.note: The burnt offering is slaughtered and burned, not released.

options.text: Nothing is burned; the blood alone matters.
options.note: Both the blood and the whole animal have their prescribed place in the ritual.

quiz.q: What does the phrase 'a pleasing aroma to the LORD' communicate about the burnt offering?

options.text: That the offering, rightly brought, is accepted by God rather than rejected.
options.note: The phrase describes divine acceptance of the sacrifice, not a literal need for scent.

options.text: That God literally needs the smell of burning meat for nourishment.
options.note: Scripture elsewhere denies that God needs anything from His creatures, Psalm 50:12-13.

options.text: That the smoke had a pleasant fragrance to the worshipers nearby.
options.note: The phrase concerns God's acceptance, not the sensory experience of the human worshipers.

options.text: It has no theological significance.
options.note: The recurring phrase is a significant marker of the offering's acceptance before the LORD.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament interpret the burnt offering and sacrifices like it?

options.text: As unable to take away sin themselves, but pointing forward to Christ's once for all sacrifice.
options.note: Hebrews 10:1-10 makes exactly this argument about the old covenant sacrifices.

options.text: As a permanent pattern Christians must continue today.
options.note: Hebrews argues the opposite, that Christ's sacrifice ends the need for repeated offerings.

options.text: As having no connection to Christ whatsoever.
options.note: The New Testament repeatedly draws direct lines from these sacrifices to Christ's work.

options.text: As myths with no historical basis.
options.note: The New Testament treats these sacrifices as a real, historical system that finds its fulfillment in Christ.

questions: The worshiper laid his own hand on the offering before it died in his place. Where do you need to identify honestly with your need for a substitute rather than trusting your own good standing before God?
The burnt offering was wholly given, nothing held back. What parts of your life do you still hold back from full surrender to God?
God provided a way for the poor to bring an acceptable offering, birds instead of a bull. How does it change your view of God to know He has always made a way for the have not as much as the have?

This sacrifice had to be repeated again and again, yet Christ's sacrifice was offered once for all. Do you live as though your standing with God still depends on repeated effort, or do you rest in what is finished?
The whole system existed so people could draw near, qorban, to God. What keeps you from drawing near to Him today, and what would it look like to approach Him through Christ this week?

translationNotes.note: The LORD called to Moses translates a Hebrew verb that opens the book, tying Leviticus directly to the end of Exodus, where the glory of the LORD had just filled the newly completed tabernacle.
Make atonement for him translates kaphar, a verb whose root sense of covering or wiping away runs through the entire sacrificial system introduced in these opening chapters.
A pleasing aroma to the LORD is an anthropomorphic phrase describing divine acceptance of the offering; it does not imply that God requires food, compare Psalm 50:8-13.
